quote: Hey. This is Bucky with Ion. I'm reaching out about the solar form you filled out online. How you doing?
stewart_read: Bucky delivers the intro correctly — company name, form reference, rapport question — but does not pause for Michelle's response before moving to verify. The 'How you doing?' is immediately followed by 'Good. I just got a few questions to get started' at 00:35, before Michelle has answered. The intros.standard success signal 'human_pause_for_response' is not met.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been reading Michelle's 'Okay' at 00:33 as sufficient and moved on. Rejected — 'Okay' is a response to being connected, not to 'How you doing?' The question hadn't been asked yet at 00:33.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky the transcript side-by-side: his 'How you doing?' and his 'Good' are separated by Michelle's 'Okay' — which was her response to the hold music, not to him. The pause is the legitimacy signal. Skipping it makes the intro feel like a script-read, not a human call.

quote: Okay. I'm driving right now. But go ahead and ask. Go ahead.
stewart_read: Michelle discloses she is driving — the mobile/away-from-home constraint that the codex's shopping_customer_protocol is built for. Bucky's 'Oh, okay' and immediate pivot to address verification treats this as a minor aside rather than a structural constraint that shapes the entire call. The correct move here is to either (a) offer a callback for when she's home, or (b) explicitly acknowledge the constraint and set expectations about what can and can't be completed live. Neither happens.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have judged that Michelle was willing to proceed and that pushing through quickly was better than losing the call to a callback. Partially valid — but the bill-photo ask at 02:13 proves the constraint was real and unresolved. The strategic read would require Bucky to have a plan for the bill; he didn't.
coaching_implication: Walk Bucky through the shopping_customer_protocol. The driving disclosure is the moment to say: 'I don't want you distracted — let me text you my info and we'll set a time when you're home.' That move locks a callback time and protects the bill-collection step. What Bucky did instead left the call ending on a loose photo promise with no appointment.

quote: That tree there, are you willing to remove it?
stewart_read: The tree-removal question surfaces a site-qualification compliance signal — Bucky is checking whether shading can be resolved. Michelle's immediate 'Yes' at 01:04 is an enthusiasm signal: she is willing to remove a tree to make solar work. This is a customer who has already mentally committed to the outcome. Bucky logs it ('Going to remove') and moves on. He does not use it as an emotional anchor — 'You're already thinking about what it takes to make this work, that's exactly the kind of homeowner our program is built for' — or tie it back to her interest in solar.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky is just running the site-qualification checklist. The tree question is procedural, not a rapport opportunity. Partially true — but the customer's willingness is the signal worth anchoring, not the tree itself.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky that 'Yes' to tree removal is a buying signal, not just a site-qualification checkbox. A one-sentence acknowledgment — 'That tells me you're serious about making this work' — costs nothing and builds the emotional case for the appointment. He walked past it.

quote: what utility company you with? A Comm Ed.
stewart_read: Bucky asks the utility-company question and then answers it himself — 'A Comm Ed' — before Michelle responds. Michelle's 'Okay' at 01:17 is ambiguous: it could be confirmation or simply an acknowledgment that she heard him. The verify item is recorded as ComEd, but the data was supplied by the rep, not confirmed by the customer. This is a data-quality gap on a required verify item.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have had ComEd pre-populated from the lead form and was confirming rather than asking. Possible — but the phrasing 'what utility company you with? A Comm Ed' is a leading question that doesn't give Michelle a chance to correct it. If the lead form had a different utility or an error, this call would book an appointment on bad data.
coaching_implication: Drill the difference between 'What utility company are you with?' (open) and 'You're with ComEd, right?' (confirming). The first gets real data. The second gets agreement. On a required verify item, the rep needs the customer's answer, not their own answer confirmed. If the lead form pre-populates utility, Bucky should say 'I have ComEd on file — is that right?' and wait for a clear yes.

quote: Yeah. That's what the regular yeah. Shingles. I think it's shingles. Yeah. But I think it needs it might need to be replaced.
stewart_read: Michelle volunteers that her roof may need replacement — the exact trigger for the cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned protocol. Bucky's response at 01:58 is 'That's fine. We have our own in house roofing.' — a one-line acknowledgment that is better than SESSION10's complete miss, but falls short of the codex's correct response pattern: bridge to the roofing team, explain the solar-prep angle, and co-route. The signal is heard; the revenue opportunity is not developed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been holding the roofing detail for the closer to develop, keeping setter scope. Possible — but the codex explicitly calls for the setter to bridge and co-route in real time, not defer. The closer won't know about the roofing need unless Bucky surfaces it in a handoff brief, which this call doesn't produce because no appointment is booked.
coaching_implication: Bucky's one-liner is a start — he knows Ion does roofing. The next step is the bridge: 'We could potentially do both — get the roof done right with solar planned into it from day one. Want me to flag that for our roofing team when we set up your appointment?' That move (a) develops the cross-sell, (b) creates a reason to book the appointment, and (c) gives Michelle a concrete next step beyond the bill photo.

quote: Do you have a credit score or your son have a credit score over six seventy?
stewart_read: Elliot is on the title and is the likely credit-qualifier — Michelle has already said she doesn't meet the threshold. Bucky asks the credit question but does not establish that Elliot must be present for the appointment. The codex's adult_child_decision_maker protocol is clear: co-decision-maker on title must be included in the appointment. Bucky captures Elliot's name at 01:23 and his likely credit qualification at 02:07 but never asks 'What time works for both of you?'
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky may not have recognized Elliot as a co-decision-maker requiring inclusion — he may have treated the title question as a data-collection item rather than a protocol trigger. This is the more likely read given that no appointment is booked at all.
coaching_implication: This is the protocol gap that would have mattered most if an appointment had been booked. Walk Bucky through the adult_child_decision_maker protocol: the moment Michelle says 'My son and myself' on the title, the appointment must be framed around both of them. 'Let's make sure Elliot is on the call with us — what time works for both of you?' That question also creates a natural path to booking, which this call never reaches.

quote: I think he he does. I know I don't, but I'm I'm I'm thinking he does.
stewart_read: Michelle self-discloses a financial limitation — she knows her own credit doesn't qualify. The hesitation in her phrasing ('I'm I'm I'm thinking') is a trust gift: she is being honest about a vulnerability rather than deflecting. Bucky's response is 'Okay. And then...' — a direct move to the next item. The codex's empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure protocol calls for acknowledgment and a bridge to value. Neither happens.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been protecting Michelle from embarrassment by not dwelling on the credit gap, moving quickly to the son's qualification as the path forward. Possible — but the codex is explicit that vulnerability disclosures require acknowledgment before the bridge. A one-sentence acknowledgment ('That's totally fine — that's exactly why we ask about everyone on the title') costs nothing and honors the trust gift.
coaching_implication: Play this moment in the 1-on-1. Michelle's hesitation is audible. Ask Bucky what he heard. Then show him the codex's empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure pattern: acknowledge specifically, bridge to how the situation is handled. Suggested intent (not verbatim): 'That's completely fine — that's actually why we ask about everyone on the title. If Elliot qualifies, we can build the whole thing around his credit.' That move acknowledges the disclosure, removes the anxiety, and pivots to the son as the path forward.

quote: I can take a picture when I get home and send it to you at this number right here that you're calling from?
stewart_read: The call ends here with no appointment, no locked callback time, and no confirmation that Elliot will be available. Michelle is proposing her own follow-up path — send a photo later — and Bucky accepts it. The button_up protocol requires appointment datetime, one-hour block, phone appointment specification, no-driving anchor, 'is that fair' handshake, and text follow-up promise. None of these are present because no appointment is being set. The call's entire value now rests on Michelle following through on a photo promise made while driving.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have judged that pushing for an appointment without the bill was premature and that securing the bill first was the right sequencing. Partially valid for a paid lead — but the correct move is to lock a callback time for when the bill arrives, not to leave the follow-up entirely to the customer.
coaching_implication: This is the call's terminal failure. Before hanging up, Bucky needed one more move: 'When do you think you'll be home? Let me put a time on the calendar for us to connect once you've sent that over.' That locks a callback, protects the lead, and gives Bucky a reason to follow up. Without it, this lead is in the customer's hands with no accountability structure.
